prompt:
You are a senior researcher in Computational Chemistry / Retrosynthesis Prediction.
Below are 10 important recent papers in this area, presented in random order. Each item contains only the paper title and a brief abstract; no lineage order, structured source-change fields, or evolutionary dynamics labels are provided.

**Frontier Question**:
RSGPT achieves 63.4% top-1 accuracy through massive pretraining but still fails on over a third of reactions, particularly stereoselective transformations and multi-step routes. What mechanism would enable retrosynthesis models to jointly reason about single-step reactivity, stereochemical outcomes, reaction conditions, and multi-step synthetic planning in a unified end-to-end framework?

[Paper 1] Retroformer: Pushing the Limits of Interpretable End-to-end Retrosynthesis Transformer
Brief Abstract: Retroformer: introduces a local attention head that jointly encodes molecular sequence and molecular graph, enabling the Transformer to focus on reactive regions while maintaining global context; achieves new SOTA for end-to-end template-free retrosynthesis.

[Paper 2] Retrosynthesis Prediction with Conditional Graph Logic Network
Brief Abstract: Conditional Graph Logic Network (GLN): a conditional graphical model built on graph neural networks that treats reaction templates as logical rules and models the joint probability of rule selection and reactant generation; achieves state-of-the-art template-based retrosynthesis accuracy on USPTO-50k.

[Paper 3] Molecular Transformer: A Model for Uncertainty-Calibrated Chemical Reaction Prediction
Brief Abstract: Molecular Transformer: replaces LSTM with self-attention Transformer for chemical reaction prediction; achieves 90.4% top-1 accuracy on forward reaction prediction and sets new SOTA for retrosynthesis; provides uncertainty calibration via beam search likelihood.

[Paper 4] Retrosynthesis prediction using an end-to-end graph generative architecture for molecular graph editing
Brief Abstract: Graph2Edits: end-to-end graph generative architecture that predicts retrosynthetic edits (bond breaking/forming) directly on the molecular graph, inspired by arrow-pushing formalism in reaction mechanisms; achieves 55.1% top-1 accuracy on USPTO-50k as a graph-native approach.

[Paper 5] Deep Retrosynthetic Reaction Prediction using Local Reactivity and Global Attention
Brief Abstract: LocalRetro: template-based retrosynthesis guided by local atom/bond reactivity predictions; a graph neural network first identifies reactive atoms and bonds, then retrieves appropriate local reaction templates; achieves 89.5% top-1 round-trip accuracy and 53.4% top-1 exact match on USPTO-50k.

[Paper 6] Root-aligned SMILES: A Tight Representation for Chemical Reaction Prediction
Brief Abstract: Root-aligned SMILES (R-SMILES): a molecular representation that aligns product and reactant SMILES strings by choosing a common root atom, reducing edit distance by 21% and enabling Transformer models to focus on chemical changes rather than syntactic differences; significantly boosts retrosynthesis prediction accuracy when combined with data augmentation.

[Paper 7] RSGPT: a generative transformer model for retrosynthesis planning pre-trained on ten billion datapoints
Brief Abstract: RSGPT: generative pretrained transformer for retrosynthesis, pretrained on 10 billion synthetically generated reaction datapoints; achieves 63.4% top-1 accuracy on USPTO-50k, substantially outperforming all previous methods; introduces reinforcement learning fine-tuning to capture product-reactant-template relationships.

[Paper 8] Learning Graph Models for Retrosynthesis Prediction
Brief Abstract: GraphRetro: a two-stage graph-based semi-template approach that first predicts graph edits (bond changes) to produce synthons, then completes synthons into full reactants by attaching leaving groups from a compact vocabulary; achieves 53.7% top-1 accuracy on USPTO-50k without reaction class.

[Paper 9] Retrosynthetic Reaction Prediction Using Neural Sequence-to-Sequence Models
Brief Abstract: First neural sequence-to-sequence model for retrosynthesis: treats retrosynthetic prediction as a machine translation problem from product SMILES to reactant SMILES using LSTM encoder-decoder; achieves 37.4% top-1 accuracy on USPTO-50k, comparable to rule-based expert systems.

[Paper 10] Computer-Assisted Retrosynthesis Based on Molecular Similarity
Brief Abstract: Template-based neural retrosynthesis using molecular fingerprint similarity: instead of generating SMILES strings, the model ranks known reaction templates by comparing molecular fingerprints of the query product to precedent reactions; achieves 74.1% top-10 accuracy on 5,000 test reactions from USPTO patent data.

Based on the frontier question and the papers above, generate a new research idea.

Return ONLY valid JSON. Do not wrap it in Markdown fences.
{
  "idea": "<short name of the proposed idea, 5-15 words>",
  "idea_description": "<150-250 words describing the full research idea>",
  "technical_plan": {
    "target_problem": "<specific problem, dataset, population, or phenomenon>",
    "core_method": "<technical method or mechanism, not a vague goal>",
    "limitation_addressed": "<frontier limitation repaired by this idea>",
    "expected_result": "<scientific result or claim if successful>"
  },
  "lineage_connection": {
    "source_ideas": ["<paper title or source idea used by this proposal>", "..."],
    "reused_parts": ["<specific method, evidence, data, or problem framing reused from the sources>", "..."],
    "changed_parts": ["<specific modified, replaced, combined, or newly added part>", "..."],
    "why_next_step": "<why this idea follows from the provided context>",
    "lineage_risk": "<most likely way this source connection could be wrong>"
  },
  "evaluation_plan": {
    "setup": "<concrete experiment, benchmark, proof, or analysis setup>",
    "baselines": ["<baseline>", "..."],
    "metrics": ["<metric>", "..."],
    "risks": ["<risk>", "..."]
  }
}
